target: data/sections.json part2b SEC-2b.7 — W001 sign-flip paragraph: steel-man detection threshold defense
status_note: The decider already integrated a substantially improved version after EXP-070 and the c2 curmudgeon review. The current text correctly leads with structural unfalsifiability and acknowledges detection-threshold legitimacy. This expansion provides targeted enhancements to close remaining vulnerabilities identified in ISS-827 and the c2 review.
kernel_of_truth: The dome's defense — "Instrument noise floor prevents measurement. Prediction remains structurally valid. Hardware limitation, not model failure" — is technically correct about how magnetometric detection thresholds work. A sub-noise measurement genuinely cannot be trusted for direction or magnitude. The sigma_distance of 7.3 uses prediction uncertainty (~0.8 nT) as its denominator, not instrument noise, so it measures statistical distance from the forecast rather than measurement reliability. These are genuinely different metrics, and conflating them (as the original pre-revision text did) would be a scientific error. The current text already avoids this conflation, but three additional points can close remaining escape hatches.

changes.description: Add the dome's own verbatim rationale to steel-man it before dismantling. Currently we paraphrase; quoting their exact language is stronger.
changes.location: After "Detection thresholds are legitimate practice in magnetometry" sentence
changes.insert_after: Detection thresholds are legitimate practice in magnetometry: when an expected signal is smaller than the instrument noise environment, no measurement can reliably confirm or deny the signal's presence, let alone its direction.
changes.insert_text: The dome states this directly: "Instrument noise floor prevents measurement. Prediction remains structurally valid. Hardware limitation, not model failure." This is the strongest version of the defense, and it deserves direct engagement.

changes.description: Note that W001's results.json entry conspicuously lacks a noise_floor field, unlike W004. This makes independent verification impossible and is itself an evidentiary gap.
changes.location: After the sentence using W004's 4.4 nT noise floor as proxy
changes.insert_after: W004's published noise floor of 4.4 nT sets the measurement scale; a −2.1 nT prediction lies well below that threshold
changes.insert_text: (W001's own results.json entry, unlike W004's, does not report a noise_floor value — the dome asks readers to accept the below_detection_threshold classification without providing the threshold that was applied)

changes.description: Add the direction_correct computation paradox. The dome's own code evaluates and stores direction_correct=false for W001 even though it claims below-noise measurements are directionless. If the dome truly believed direction was meaningless below noise, it would not compute the field.
changes.location: After the sentence about scoring logic classifying snr_sufficient=false as below_detection_threshold
changes.current_text: The scoring logic classifies any result with snr_sufficient: false as below_detection_threshold regardless of what direction_correct reports — even when the observation is 5.83 nT from the prediction with the wrong sign.
changes.replacement_text: The scoring logic classifies any result with snr_sufficient: false as below_detection_threshold regardless of what direction_correct reports — even when the observation is 5.83 nT from the prediction with the wrong sign. Yet the dome's own code computes and stores direction_correct: false for W001. If below-noise measurements truly carry no directional information, the field would be meaningless to evaluate. The dome's code disagrees with the dome's classification: it considers direction evaluable enough to compute but not evaluable enough to count.

changes.description: Make the precision derivation explicit so readers can verify it.
changes.location: The sentence about 0.8 nT implied prediction precision
changes.current_text: The dome's ≈0.8 nT implied prediction precision (derived from the sigma_distance denominator)
changes.replacement_text: The dome's ≈0.8 nT implied prediction precision (computed: |3.73 − (−2.1)| / 7.2875 = 5.83 / 7.2875 ≈ 0.80 nT, the sigma_distance denominator)

replacement_html: The sign-flip the dome does not count. W001 ("Lunar Transit Magnetic Anomaly — HUA") predicted −2.1 nT at Huancayo station during a lunar transit — a signal below the instrument noise floor for standard ground magnetometry. Detection thresholds are legitimate practice in magnetometry: when an expected signal is smaller than the instrument noise environment, no measurement can reliably confirm or deny the signal's presence, let alone its direction. The dome states this directly: "Instrument noise floor prevents measurement. Prediction remains structurally valid. Hardware limitation, not model failure." This is the strongest version of the defense, and it deserves direct engagement. The structural problem is that the W001 prediction was unfalsifiable before any data arrived. W004's published noise floor of 4.4 nT sets the measurement scale (W001's own results.json entry, unlike W004's, does not report a noise_floor value — the dome asks readers to accept the below_detection_threshold classification without providing the threshold that was applied); a −2.1 nT prediction lies well below that threshold, meaning any observation — positive, negative, or near-zero — would receive below_detection_threshold classification regardless of what the instruments registered. When the observed value came in at +3.73 nT (wrong sign; sigma_distance 7.3 relative to the ≈0.8 nT prediction uncertainty; direction_correct: false), the scoring system performed exactly as designed: auto_verdict: "below_detection_threshold", counts_against_model: false. The scoring logic classifies any result with snr_sufficient: false as below_detection_threshold regardless of what direction_correct reports — even when the observation is 5.83 nT from the prediction with the wrong sign. Yet the dome's own code computes and stores direction_correct: false for W001. If below-noise measurements truly carry no directional information, the field would be meaningless to evaluate. The dome's code disagrees with the dome's classification: it considers direction evaluable enough to compute but not evaluable enough to count. The issue is not that the dome ignores a reliable measurement; it is that the dome makes predictions small enough that no measurement can reliably test them. The dome's ≈0.8 nT implied prediction precision (computed: |3.73 − (−2.1)| / 7.2875 = 5.83 / 7.2875 ≈ 0.80 nT, the sigma_distance denominator) is five times smaller than the instruments' noise floor — a precision-to-capability gap that structurally protects every below-noise prediction from falsification. The distinction to draw is not between a "detection threshold" (legitimate) and a "falsification shield" (illegitimate): the threshold mechanism itself is standard. It is between predictions that happen to land near noise incidentally and predictions systematically placed below the noise floor, guaranteeing that the entire below-noise category can never count against the model regardless of what the instruments record.
holes_addressed: ISS-827: sigma_distance vs SNR conflation — already fixed in current text; this expansion adds the direction_correct computation paradox as additional evidence
c2 hole: noise_floor_value_absent — added parenthetical noting W001 has no published noise_floor
c2 hole: dome_defense_not_preempted — added verbatim dome rationale before dismantling
c2 stronger_argument: dome stores direction_correct even for below-threshold — integrated as the code-disagrees-with-classification point
new_evidence_added: Dome verbatim rationale from wins.html: "Instrument noise floor prevents measurement. Prediction remains structurally valid. Hardware limitation, not model failure."
W001 results.json entry lacks noise_floor field (confirmed from ISS-705 independent re-fetch)
direction_correct computed and stored as false even though snr_sufficient=false

anticipated_objections.objection: Using W004's noise floor (4.4 nT) as proxy for W001 is invalid — different stations, different dates, different conditions.
anticipated_objections.response: True in principle, but the dome itself classifies W001 as below_detection_threshold without publishing W001's noise floor value. We use 4.4 nT as a reference point, not as a proven W001 threshold. The argument holds at any noise floor above 2.1 nT — even a generous 2.5 nT threshold would make the prediction sub-noise. The dome's refusal to publish W001's noise floor prevents anyone (including us) from doing better.

anticipated_objections.objection: Computing direction_correct is just good engineering — log everything, even if some fields are inapplicable in some contexts. Doesn't mean the dome thinks direction is reliable below noise.
anticipated_objections.response: Fair point about engineering practice. But the dome's results.json schema treats direction_correct and snr_sufficient as the two inputs to auto_verdict (pass = both true). The field is not incidental logging — it is one half of the verdict formula. The dome's code evaluates it as decision-relevant, then discards it when it returns the wrong answer. If the field were purely diagnostic, the schema would label it differently or omit it from the verdict logic.

anticipated_objections.objection: The 0.8 nT precision is an artifact of how sigma_distance is computed, not a claimed prediction precision. The dome may not claim ±0.8 nT accuracy.
anticipated_objections.response: The sigma_distance field appears in the dome's own results.json with 4 decimal places (7.2875), computed from prediction_value, observed_value, and an implied uncertainty. If the dome does not intend the denominator as a precision claim, the field is meaningless — a ratio with an arbitrary denominator proves nothing. Either sigma_distance means something (in which case the denominator IS the prediction uncertainty) or it is decorative (in which case the dome's entire quantitative scoring framework is hollow).
